questions.question: According to goal-setting theory (Locke & Latham), what type of goals lead to highest performance?

options.text: Easy, achievable goals that ensure success and build confidence
Vague, flexible goals like 'do your best' that reduce pressure
Specific, challenging goals that stretch the person but remain achievable with effort
Impossible 'stretch goals' that push people beyond their limits

questions.explanation: Decades of goal-setting research show that specific, challenging goals improve performance on 90% of tasks — significantly more than easy or vague goals. The key condition: the person must have sufficient knowledge and skill to pursue the goal. The sweet spot is a 6-7/10 difficulty level: uncomfortable enough to activate motivation, but not so hard it feels impossible and triggers demoralization.

questions.question: What is the key difference between process goals and outcome goals, and why does it matter for weekly coaching focus?

options.text: Process goals are behaviors fully within your control (e.g., 'Follow my eating plan 5 days'); outcome goals are results partially outside your control (e.g., 'Lose 2 pounds'). Process goals build self-efficacy because you can guarantee success through effort.
Process goals are short-term; outcome goals are long-term. There's no real difference in effectiveness.
Process goals are easier and less motivating; outcome goals are harder and more inspiring, so you should always focus on outcomes.
Process goals are for beginners; advanced clients should focus exclusively on outcome goals.

questions.explanation: Process goals focus on actions completely within your control. You can't control whether you lose 2 pounds this week (water weight, hormones, metabolism vary), but you CAN control whether you follow your eating plan 5 days. This controllability reduces anxiety and builds self-efficacy through reliable mastery experiences. Use outcome goals for long-term vision; use process goals for weekly accountability.

questions.question: Why do proximal goals (small, near-term steps) build self-efficacy more effectively than distant outcome goals?

options.text: Proximal goals are easier, so people feel less pressure and perform better.

Proximal goals create frequent mastery experiences — successfully completing challenging tasks — which is the strongest source of self-efficacy. Each small win activates dopamine and reinforces 'I can do this.'
Proximal goals don't actually build self-efficacy — only big accomplishments do that.
Proximal goals work only for people with low confidence; high performers should skip them.

questions.explanation: Mastery experiences (successfully completing challenging tasks) are the most powerful source of self-efficacy (Bandura). Proximal goals break large goals into achievable steps, creating frequent opportunities for mastery. Each completion activates dopamine reward and builds the neural pathway: 'I follow through on commitments.' Distant goals provide no feedback or reinforcement for months, allowing motivation to decay.

questions.question: What are implementation intentions ('if-then' planning), and why do they improve goal attainment?

options.text: Detailed plans that specify every possible scenario and eliminate all flexibility

Pre-commitment strategies that specify when, where, and how you'll act (e.g., 'If it's Monday morning, then I'll put on running shoes after my alarm'). They work by offloading decision-making from the moment of action (when willpower is weak) to the planning phase (when motivation is high).
Goals that have backup plans in case you fail
A planning method with no research support — it's just trendy coaching jargon

questions.explanation: Implementation intentions are if-then plans that pre-decide when, where, and how you'll act. Meta-analyses show they significantly improve goal attainment (medium-to-large effects). They work because they remove in-the-moment decision fatigue. Instead of deciding every morning whether to exercise, you've already decided: 'If it's MWF at 6am, then I put on running shoes.' This automation increases follow-through.

questions.question: A client says: 'Everything feels so overwhelming right now. I don't even know where to start with goal-setting.' What's the most effective coaching response?

options.text: 'Let's just pick something small and start — action creates clarity.'
'If everything is overwhelming, maybe you're not ready for coaching. Come back when you have clarity.'
'It sounds like you're feeling really overwhelmed. Before we talk about goals, tell me — what's taking up the most space in your mind right now?'
'Here's a simple goal-setting template. Fill this out and we'll review it next time.'

questions.explanation: Reflect the emotion (overwhelmed) and explore what's driving it before jumping to goal-setting. Pushing someone who's already overwhelmed increases resistance. Often, exploring the overwhelm reveals what actually matters most — and that becomes the meaningful starting point for goals. Option 1 ignores their emotional state. Option 2 abandons them when they most need support. Option 4 adds more tasks to an overwhelmed person.

title: Goal Setting and Action Planning - Quiz
